segments.text: Welcome to the Star with Ryan.
So without any further ado, I'd like to welcome the guest tonight, Ryan Buckley from FitCo.
It's a funny, I've introduced a few people that the only thing they were scared of the
It was just walking through the crowd of people screaming and yelling.
It was quite scary for some.
Luckily, I've walked on a few runways before as a model, so I'm a little used to that, but
this is definitely a friendlier crowd than I've seen in the past, so thank you.
Well, so first of all, thanks for being here.
And why don't you just kind of start off and tell everyone the elevator pitch, what's FitCo.
Give us the basics of that, and then we're going to start from there.
So FitCo is a product that is placed right on brand and retailer websites.
So what we do is when you are shopping for jeans, it's now men's and women's.
And say you are on a site like Hudson Jeans, and you see hundreds, if not thousands, of
different options of what to buy, and you have no idea which is going to fit.
We don't need measurements, and then from there, we personalize the whole experience.
So we say, you know, out of these 800 jeans that are offered, here are the 15 that are going
to fit you and what size to buy in them.
And so what's the, like, what's the inspiration?
Like, now being a model, I'm sure you're trying on boatloads of jeans that don't fit well,
and you have clips in the back to hold them up and pins everywhere to make them look perfect.
So, like, is that where it all started?
Was that just kind of that, just experiencing it yourself?
Yeah, it was from years of modeling.
So modeling was my introduction to fashion industry.
And so I spent the first six years or so from, like, my junior year in college until after college
modeling for companies all around the world, one of the biggest being here in Nordstrom.
I was those two legs you see in all those denim images for years.
I would be on set, and the jeans would look perfect from the front, and then if I turned around,
I would be completely exposed in the back.
I would have clips and pins just holding the jeans together so they, you know, look good from the front,
but in the back they were completely torn up.
And at the same time, off set, I was listening to retailers complain about the online return rate.
And I thought, you know, there is this huge void here.
What people are seeing in product images and what they're getting at the door is just not the same.
And then at the same time, you're putting all of these products online, and people can't try them on.
So how are they going to find jeans that fit?
So that's where it all really started.
And, you know, the genesis of it all came from thinking, hey, this is a big problem.
I've been given a really interesting, you know, view into it.
And if I don't do this, someone else is going to.
And the reason why I, you know, got that, like, braveness to start was just being concerned
that someone else was going to do it and I was going to miss out.
So that was really the deciding factor for me.
I was like, you got to do it.
You got to do it now.
Yeah, or else somebody else is going to do it.
And what's really interesting and what I really like about this is that you've never done remotely anything.
So, like, tell us, like, that's a huge step.
Like, I mean, it's a huge step to just, like, you know what, I'm going to do this.
I don't know what the hell I'm doing, but I'm going to figure it out.
So tell us, like, how, like, did you have a boyfriend there?
I mean, how did that, like, talking about your inner circle, like, I'm sure you talked to
some people that said, hey, I'm going to do this.
There's no way in the hell that's going to work.
I mean, I'm sure that happened.
I wish my husband's here tonight and I wish I could say that he was the big help here,
but he was a football player, so he did not come with a technical background.
But, you know, it really did come from, he was inspiring to me.
It was a lot of people around me that were just, like, why not you?
You know, why do you have to have the perfect resume to start a company?
And so I graduated with a degree in political science and ocean conservation studies,
Yeah, so it has nothing to do with startups, nothing to do with technology,
and also nothing to do with modeling.
So I think, you know, you just kind of, you set the foundation, you do with, you know,
you go where you're passionate at the moment, and then you see where life takes you.
And so I was able to start modeling, and from there I never thought, like,
hey, if I start modeling, I'm going to start a startup, but you discover these problems.
And just like you were saying in the intro, if you find a big problem
and you start realizing this is a problem for multiple people,
you may have discovered something special.
And so kind of look into that and lean into that.
And what was the, like, so when was the month that you had the idea, like, I'm going to do this?
What month of the year was that?
You know, it was in 2014 is when I actually decided to start working on it.
I don't know the month that it was.
We launched officially in February of 2015, but we really were building just kind of internally in 2014.
So let's just say it was July of 2014.
So you're sitting there with some friends in July of 2014 saying, hey, I'm going to do this thing.
And it is now January of 2018.
If you look back now, what is the biggest surprise for Ryan about you personally?
Like, what did you learn about Ryan that you were like, holy shit, I didn't know I could do that?
Or I didn't know I was that good at that or I was that bad at that.
What was the big surprise for you?
I think that, you know, that's a great question, but I think that being naive is a huge strength.
And I think that if you had asked me in the beginning stages, like, you don't have any experience in this, how is that going to play out?
I would have been like, ooh, that's going to be a detriment.
And to me, it was probably the biggest strength I had going into this because I knew the problem really, really intimately.
I knew where I wanted the product to go.
I had no idea technically what it was going to take to build it, and so that was never a hurdle for me.
And so I just knew what consumers wanted.
I knew what retailers and brands wanted, and then my amazing dev teams had to make it work.
But I didn't go into it thinking, ooh, this is going to be really hard to solve.
And so therefore, you know, not actually wanting to try.
It's just like, I know we can do this.
I know where it needs to go.
You guys figure out how to do it.
So then basically not being, not knowing to do it actually was a strength of that.
And where, what is the thing that you've had to learn, like, you've had to learn how to do, like, you've never managed people before.
You're going to want a team, like, what, eight or 10, 12 people now?
So what things have you learned that you just, one, had to learn, but two, actually turned out to be pretty good at it.

You know, I think the thing you have to learn when you are a first-time founder and then the CEO, especially of a company our size, which is still pretty small, is that you have to wear every single hat.
And so everyone on our team wears a lot of hats, but as the CEO, you know, you are the people manager, which is something that I had to learn and I'm still learning how to do.

In the beginning stages, you do all the invoicing, you do all the financing, you do all the HR, you work with legal, and then you have to do all the sales, you have to do all the product development, and then you have to do all the strategy.
And so there is a lot to learn, and I think the thing that I got good at in the beginning is starting to segment my day.
And so I'll spend certain amounts of my day focusing on one thing, and I really try to stay there, because if I start, you know, being like that ping-pong ball, I'm never going to get things done.
And so I got really good at just segmenting and being like, right now I'm going to do this, and then I'm going to move on to this next part of my day.
Yeah, so you just learned how to kind of manage your day, the time in your day.
And then turning it off at the end of the day, that's something I've really had to learn to do.
If you, do you ever really turn off?
You don't turn off the stress, but I think you can turn off the, I,
I live by this theory that the best thing to alleviate stress is hard work.
And it's hard at that point if you, startups come with an abundant amount of stress.
And so it's hard to turn it off, but if you can be like, hey, you know, have a super productive eight to nine hours,
ten hours during your day, and then turn it off and go to sleep.
Because if you don't have those few hours off, you're not going to be productive the next day.
And not that you have to stop, you know, thinking about it, but you have to turn off the stress, turn off the pressure.
That's very hard for some and extraordinarily easy for others.
So where, where is your, your head at today in looking ahead, say, even a year, 18 months out?
Like, where do you think that you're going to, you think you're going to have to learn as a company, not as Orion, but as a company?
Where do you think you guys are going to have to get better at the next 12 to 18 months to be successful?
We're kind of right at this cliff right now where we're going to have an explosion of growth, most likely in the next quarter and quarter, you know, next two quarters probably.
And so it's really understanding that we need to keep our consumer at the forefront of our minds.
We have our brand and retail partners, and we also have the customers who use the product.
And so, you know, I talked to the team a lot about it, but there are a ton of really cool places our technology could go.
But if we're not hearing the demand from our brands and our retailers and our customers, then we can't go there right now.
And so we have to stay really, really focused on what the end goal is.
And, and tell us about the experience of raising money.
That's, I'm sure, I'm sure you have a couple stories there, but one, the elephant in the room is your woman raising money, which is, as you know, not an easy process.
It's what is like 4% of the money gets to women-led companies.
Tell us, like, what was your philosophy around that?
Me being an outsider would say not knowing is probably huge here because you didn't have any preconceived, you know, notions like, well, this is how it should work.
But how did, how did that pan out for you?
And obviously you raised money, which is great.
Again, it was being naive and I didn't go in as the underdog.

So I think if I went in knowing all these stats and I went in knowing how hard it was for other women to raise money, I would have felt like the underdog and I would have come in and, you know, maybe had that lens come into the process and been more concerned about it.
I wasn't concerned because I knew the problem and I knew that, you know, I never even thought about me being a woman trying to solve it.
I thought, I have the position to solve this from.
And I talk to other women founders about this all the time that 2% of money may go to you, but lead with your numbers.
You have to lead with your numbers.
And being a woman is great and it's a really big and it's a powerful thing.
But these people just want to make money just like you do.
And so make it irresistible to them.
Let them know if you invest in me, this is how much money you're going to make and this is how long it's going to take and this is why you should.
And they're going to invest in you.
So always lead with your numbers.
And then not the made up ones, the real ones.

I mean, the relationship, this is such a good point because so many people, you see that on, you know, TV shows and all this kind of stuff, they go in and they pitch these big VCs and they have these ridiculous projections.
And they don't realize that the relationship with your investor is going to be one of the most important relationships you have in business.
And they are keeping you guys alive or not.
And so if you start off by doing projections that aren't accurate, you're starting off in a dishonest way and it's not going to be a good relationship.
So let them know where you're at.
Let them know what you're not good at and what they can bring to the table and it's going to be a good relationship.
Yeah, it's as hard to break up with your VC as it is to get divorced.
So, yeah, don't take that lightly at all.
And if you could, you know, wave your magic wand and start over again, would you change anything?
With funding or with the company?
No, just the start of, just starting what you did.
Would there be some things you're like, shit, I wish I would have done this, would have saved us six months or would have saved us?
Yeah, I mean, I think you can always, hindsight's 20-20 as we all know.
We could have stayed on task more.
Being a first-time founder and a first-time CEO, there are definitely things that we got excited about and we went down certain paths that didn't end up being the direction we needed to go.
But those are all just lessons learned.
It just reminds you, you know, bring it back.
Like, you may think this is where the product needs to go.
But again, if the people who are paying you for it aren't saying that, listen to that and listen to what they want and what they find to be valuable and go that direction.
You know, I listened to some of the stuff written about you and some of the stuff, you know, actually did a little research.
One of the things you talked about was venture capital is debt.
It's a really interesting way to think about it, but just explain what you've already said before, but just explain what you consider that theory.
The theory is that any money you take, whether it's venture capital money or whatever, it's actually debt.
Yeah, this is something that I hadn't heard before coming into this process.
And when I started talking about it, people kept being like, what are you saying?
And I was wondering what they were saying because they were talking about that if you go out and you get this, you know, series.
So we've gone through series A, but series B is usually where you get the big sums of money, so the 20 million plus.
And they're all talking about how, you know, when are you going to go for series B?
When are you going to get that big number?
And I was thinking, why would I get that big number?
Why wouldn't I just work on being profitable?
And it was this big, you know, just like, whoa, there's something totally off in this industry.
And it's that founders are constantly trying to go for the big number because they think somehow that's going to make their company more valuable.
In the olden days, valuations were constantly tied to how much money you raised.
And now people are realizing you can't do that anymore.
You have to tie valuations to how much money your company is actually making.
So for me, knowing that and knowing that's how I wanted to do our startup, I realized the best way to get profitable is to not get myself
in that big of a hole with investments.
So all I wanted to do is think about how little money could I raise to get us to be cash flow positive.
And that's what we're still doing.
And it's worked out well for us.
I don't want the 20 million because I have to make up that 20 million to even get, you know, to be in power.
Yeah, so what's important to remember there is when you raise 20 million, the investor wants more than 20 million, FYI.
So let's just say they want 60 million, which would actually only be a 3X, which they don't.
But if they get 3X, that's 60 million.
So therefore, your company has to be 100 million before you even get, like, any money as a founder.
Yeah, and that's the thing that people don't talk about.
If you're the founder and the CEO, you're not making a lot of money in your salary.
So you're betting that this thing's going to be sold.
So you're taking a lower salary in these years that you're working really hard and hoping you're going to sell it.
And if you have now given away a ton of your company because you wanted that big number because it looked good in press
and you thought it was going to make your company cooler, when you do end up selling, if you end up selling,
you're giving away a majority of it.
And now you're not going to get that payday either.
Yeah, it's really important that you bootstrap as long as you humanly possibly can.
It's so much better for almost every situation.
I say almost because there is some situations.
And so, you know, if you could kind of bring anyone into the company, what would be the most important person to you
at this stage of bringing someone into the company?
That's an easy question for me to answer right now because we are doing interviews tomorrow.
So, so far, I have been the only salesperson on the team.
We now have two of our employees helping me out, which is awesome.
But we have a huge pipeline.
And as you guys know, there are tons of brands and tons of retailers out there.
And I cannot possibly do it all.
So, we are hiring a salesperson, which is going to be awesome.
And it's why I think we're going to explode in the next few months.
So, my dev team is anxious about that but also excited.
And when did you decide, like, there's no way I can sell?
Mainly when I heard from our investor for about the 200th time, like, so when are you going to get more people on board?
And I was like, I cannot be on the plane this much.
I can't possibly meet with this many people.
So, we have to, we just have to widen that net.
Plus, he probably doesn't want you gone that much either.
Yeah, and I don't want to be gone that much either.
So, tell us your experience, you know, especially early days.
And when I say early, the first 12 months is really crazy, it's hard, and it's very unpredictable.
Like, tell us where the things that were the big surprises for you in growing the company at those early, early stages.
And I mean when it's you and a couple people, that kind of stuff.
Like, where were the surprises for you that, like, if you're not going to start a company tomorrow, you're like, hey, these are the things that caught me off guard last time.
I'm not going to let this happen this time.
Like, what are those things that you can help share with the audience and other people that have ideas to start a company?
What were the things that kind of caught you off guard that, it was like, here's some things that I should have done better or I just did well.
It's not that you couldn't do it well.
I think you have to have a ton of confidence to start a startup.
And I would have said I had a lot of confidence going into this.
But I think when you're starting something that's so unknown, you bring in a lot of people to help you.
And so, you have to really look at people who you bring on board early on.
And this is something that I was pretty careful with.
And you have to know that people who you bring on are going to be with the company for a long time.
So, make sure they're adding value.
And the people who I started this company with are people who are still in my life today and are doing amazing things for our company.
So, I see two of them every single day and it's awesome.
But surround yourself with a team of people.
It's just like what President Lincoln did, you know, a team of rivals.
So, surround yourself with people who not only think differently but are complete experts in things that you're terrible at.
So, I didn't need someone who had my background, who had my expertise.
I needed someone who would come in who's made profitable businesses, someone who had a technical background.
And so, that's what I really implore people to do is surround yourself with people who are really strong where you're weak.

I mean, the other thing too is when you bring people on, you know, you're going to have board members and you're going to have contractors, you're going to have employees and you have to learn how to structure all of that.
So, I didn't know how to do any of that.
But, you know, that's another thing is really think about who's going to help you through that process because people are always going to be the most expensive thing in your company.
And so, you have to make sure you can stay lean there but keep really happy employees too.
And so, talk to people who have done it before and figure out how to structure that for your own company.

You know, having the second person they hire or the third person they hire or the fourth even, you think about how much effect they have because they're 25% of your company or 30% of your company, just one hire.
And so, you have to, in the beginning, I think you really have to bring people in who, you know, can do what you cannot do.
But it's also all about the culture of the company.
You guys have to enjoy coming to work every single day.

And, you know, once you guys grow a little bit more, you can start to hire people who are less of a culture fit but more of just like they're really kick-ass at what they do and they know they're going to help build the company.
But in the beginning, you have to surround yourself with people who are going to want to be on this ride with you because it is not always fun.
You know, I can't be super positive every single day and so you have to have people who are like, hey, this is a grind and we're enjoying the grind.
It's not all, you know, what you see on movies and TV shows.
It's not that glamorous and so they have to really want to be in the trenches with you.
Where is the, like, what is the thing that makes you get up in the morning?
Like, what's the thing, is it the solving of the problem?
What is the thing that gets you up every morning, gets you to work besides a cup of coffee?
The thing that keeps me going because, you know, everyone knows about the troughs of despair as a startup founder.

But what keeps me going is if you've built a product that you know is actively solving a problem and it's not a problem that just you think exists, it's a problem that has been validated across the industry.
You also know that the only way it's not going to be successful is if you don't let it be successful.
And so every day I get up and no matter what's happened the day before or the week before with partners or clients or whoever, you have to keep fighting.
Because you know in the end your product will solve the problem and you just have to keep fighting to get it in the place that it needs to be to do that.
And so that's really what keeps me going every day is, you know, we see our competitors building these products.
We see that this is a huge need and we have to outlast them.
We have to be more agile.
We have to be faster and we just have to outlast them.
And, you know, talking about competitors, I think it's really easy to get intimidated by them.
But what I always do is I have a background.
I was a swimmer in college and so I'm very competitive anyways.
But I always think you can't be scared of these companies that are bigger than you because they just have people at the forefront of them too.
And so those people have fears and they have weaknesses and, you know, they have stress too.
And they're not near as nimble.
You have to be super nimble and agile.
Where is the, again, going back to the scenario of you and I are starting a company tomorrow, what would be the thing that you would say, this is the must-have first thing we have to do to focus on?
What is the first thing we focus on with our idea, in your opinion?
What is the most critical thing to focus on?
And understanding how whoever you're solving this problem for thinks about the problem.
So for fit code, I understood the consumer side.
You know, we've all shopped online.
We all know you don't know what's going to fit.
What I didn't understand is that the fashion industry is really, really resistant to innovation right now.
So technology, innovation, it's things that they haven't excelled at in the past.
And so they're resistant to it because they just don't understand it.
And so I didn't know that going into it, that we were going to have to, you know, pull these companies along with us and teach them
that, hey, you know, we know where the industry's at.
We all hear about all these stores closing and people buying bankrupt.
And you can sell things online.
You can do it really, really successfully with really good margins.
And you have to understand how the people who you're solving this for think about the problem.
So you can really position the product for them.
Yeah, it's really being able to put yourself in their shoes and empathy for the customer, like understanding how painful it is for them.
And at the same time, they're just a person, too, who's trying to keep their job.
So if you can give them a product that can help them keep their job, they're going to want to buy it.
Because they are, some of them are wanting to keep their job.
I mean, they're concerned about losing their job.
And in your business, in the fashion business, what are some things that are unique to the fashion business that are maybe not in the technology business?
Like when you're dealing with Joe Jeans or, right, what is unique to them that maybe not is unique to software company?
Again, that it's their, since they're a fashion company, they hold their product highest.
And so it's not always about, you know, increasing their sales dramatically or being that innovator or being at the forefront of technology.
What they're caring about is the creative process and the creative side of their product.
And so it's saying, hey, you guys own that.
For, you know, any of these denim brands, we go in and we say, you guys make awesome jeans.
That's what you guys are experts at.
What we're really good at is getting your customers to know which of your jeans to buy.
And so it's working with these companies to say, hey, you know, you can't do this without us.
And it's really good to take these small incremental bets on companies like FitCode that can come in and say, you have all these jeans.
Let's get them on more people.
Let's show people how easy it is to find jeans that fit and using a product like ours.
And you've had 100 plus thousand users, right?
That data has got to be getting super valuable.
So we have, we just found out, I think it was today or yesterday, that we have almost 200,000 users now.
I would have said 100,000 if you asked me this like two days ago.
So we're growing quickly, but the data is amazing.
So we have data that people have never seen before because, you know, all these brands and retailers have the data of people shopping on their site.
But suddenly we're able to say, we know the body shapes and the shopping habits of all your customers.
We also know how all of your products map to that.
So we can say, you know, for a company like Nordstrom, 30% of your population looks like this.
And you are not selling jeans that fit them.
And here's what you can sell.
It doesn't fit your 30% of your audience.
So they're going all the way back to manufacturing saying, hey, we don't need that one anymore.
I mean, this is where my background in modeling really comes in is that a lot of people don't understand that a denim company makes their entire line off one fit model.
